figures.gloss: The Pedi founder (c. 1885–1948) who rose through the Zionist and Apostolic Faith streams, received his healing commission, and in 1924 founded the Zion Christian Church in the Limpopo hills — today, with its St Engenas sister church (the star and the dove), the largest African-initiated church in southern Africa, numbering in the millions and led since 1975 by his grandson Bishop Barnabas Lekganyane.
figures.facet: The founder as dynasty: where mission churches rotate clergy, the ZCC binds its episcopate to Lekganyane's bloodline — succession disputes at each death (1948, 1967) split the church along the founder's sons, star from dove.
figures.provenance: E.K. Lukhaimane, 'The Zion Christian Church of Ignatius (Engenas) Lekganyane, 1924 to 1948' (1980 thesis); Allan Anderson, 'Zion and Pentecost' (2000).

figures.gloss: The ZCC's holy city east of Polokwane, on farmland the church acquired in the 1940s — destination of the Easter pilgrimage that draws millions of badge-wearing members in the largest regular religious gathering in southern Africa, blessed in person by presidents who come courting the bishop.
figures.facet: Zion transposed onto African ground: the name descends from Dowie's Zion City, Illinois, through the biblical mountain to a Limpopo farm — a holy center whose annual gravitational pull makes the church's scale physically visible once a year.
figures.provenance: Anderson (2000); the Easter pilgrimage is extensively documented in South African press and scholarship.

figures.gloss: The silver star (dove, for St Engenas ZCC) pinned on green cloth, worn constantly by members — issued with membership, blessed, and widely held to carry protection for the wearer at work, on the road, and against unseen attack.
figures.facet: Membership made wearable and protective at once: the badge is simultaneously an identity document, a uniform, and a blessed object — church bureaucracy and apotropaic power in a single pin.
figures.provenance: Jean Comaroff, 'Body of Power, Spirit of Resistance' (1985) on Zionist protective insignia; Anderson (2000).

figures.gloss: The ZCC's male dance-praise: uniformed groups in khaki and white manyanyatha boots leaping and stamping the earth in disciplined unison, a percussive worship that shakes the ground at Moria and in township congregations every week.
figures.facet: Praise as drill and as power: the mokhukhu turns the migrant-labor world's uniforms and regimentation into an instrument of the Spirit — order itself made ecstatic.
figures.provenance: Anderson (2000); documented in South African ethnomusicology of ZCC worship.

figures.gloss: Zionist congregations run on prophets: members seized by the Spirit (Moya) who diagnose the causes of sickness and misfortune and prescribe blessed water, tea and coffee, sanctified papers, cords, and conduct — the healing consultation is the church's daily front door.
figures.facet: Sundkler's classic finding: the Zionist prophet is the functional successor of the diviner — the same clients bring the same afflictions, but the revealing agent is now the Holy Spirit rather than the ancestral shades or the bones.
figures.provenance: Bengt Sundkler, 'Bantu Prophets in South Africa' (1948/1961); Comaroff (1985) on Spirit-diagnosis among the Tshidi.

edges.note: Parallel founding prophets of the two great African-initiated church families of the same generation — both streams seeded in part by American divine-healing networks (Faith Tabernacle in Nigeria, Dowie's Zion in South Africa), both re-rooting Christianity in African healing pragmatics.
Founder and holy city: Moria is the Lekganyane seat, and the Easter pilgrimage renews the dynasty's covenant with its millions annually.

GOLD SEAM (kin wave): South African Zionism fused Dowie's Zion City healing movement with Azusa-derived Pentecostalism when the Apostolic Faith Mission arrived in 1908 — Lekganyane rose through exactly this Zionist-Apostolic stream before founding the ZCC.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): Comaroff documents Zionist Spirit-diagnosis answering the same client demand the dingaka's divining bones once served — the consultation survives, re-sourced to the Holy Spirit.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): Sundkler's Zulu Zionist prophets receive a calling-sickness and a diagnostic office patterned on the isangoma, whose power flows from the amadlozi — the Spirit takes the structural seat of the shades.
Zion relocated onto the believer's own continent — a Limpopo holy city and an Ethiopian promised land, two African re-plantings of the same biblical mountain.
Worn, blessed regalia carrying protection for the body of the believer — the badge and the shirt as membership and armor in one garment.

dossier.essence: The Zionist churches — the amaZioni — are the largest church family in southern Africa, descended from the first Zionist baptisms at Wakkerstroom in 1904 (an outpost of John Alexander Dowie's Zion City, Illinois) crossed with Azusa-derived Pentecostalism after the Apostolic Faith Mission arrived in 1908. Their greatest single body is the Zion Christian Church, founded by Engenas Lekganyane in 1924 and led from Moria in Limpopo by his bloodline ever since; South African censuses make the ZCC the country's largest single denomination, with the wider Zionist family counted in the many millions.
dossier.cosmos: The worldview is Christian atop a Bantu substrate that remains diagnostic: sickness and misfortune have agents — witchcraft, evil spirits, the displeasure of the unseen — and the Holy Spirit (Moya) now performs the revealing work. God heals through prayer and blessed substances rather than the diviner's bones, but as Sundkler classically argued, the Zionist prophet occupies the structural seat of the diviner, and the ancestral shades remain a felt presence at the church's edges. Death opens onto resurrection hope in broadly Christian terms.
dossier.practice: Congregational life runs on the healing consultation: prophets (baprofeti) seized by the Spirit diagnose affliction and prescribe blessed water, tea and coffee, sanctified papers and cords, and conduct. Members wear the blessed badge — silver star on green cloth for the ZCC, dove for the St Engenas branch — abstain from alcohol and tobacco, dance the stamping mokhukhu drill-praise, and converge on Moria each Easter in the largest regular religious gathering in southern Africa.
dossier.canon: The Bible alone; the churches produce no supplementary scripture, and their distinctive teaching lives in oral preaching, prophetic utterance, and the episcopal authority of the founder's line.
dossier.reflects: Functional succession as the engine of conversion — a Christianity that won its millions by taking over, rather than dismissing, the healing consultation the diviner had always provided.
sources: Bengt Sundkler, 'Bantu Prophets in South Africa' (1961)
Jean Comaroff, 'Body of Power, Spirit of Resistance: The Culture and History of a South African People' (1985)
Allan H. Anderson, 'Zion and Pentecost: The Spirituality and Experience of Pentecostal and Zionist/Apostolic Churches in South Africa' (2000)

links.cite: Bengt Sundkler, 'Bantu Prophets in South Africa' (1961)
Jean Comaroff, 'Body of Power, Spirit of Resistance: The Culture and History of a South African People' (1985)
